questions.question: What architectural difference distinguishes an action-conditioned world model (Genie 3) from a pure video generation model (Sora 2)?
options: Pure video generators condition on a prompt at t=0 and roll out; action-conditioned world models take a latent or explicit action per frame so the user can steer the rollout mid-generation
Action-conditioned models are smaller
Only the training datasets differ
World models only work on 3D scenes
questions.explanation: Sora 2 is autoregressive on spacetime tokens; its prompt sets the scene but you cannot change direction mid-rollout. Genie 3 infers or takes a latent action at each step and conditions the next-frame prediction on it, letting the user interact with the simulated world. This interactivity is what makes a model a 'world simulator' rather than a video generator.

questions.question: Divided attention runs temporal attention O(H·W · T²) then spatial attention O(T · (H·W)²) per block. For T=8 and H·W=256 (a 16×16 spatial grid), which option best describes the compute savings over full joint attention O((T·H·W)²)?
options: About 2× fewer operations — the savings are modest at small video sizes
Exactly 4× fewer — divided attention always halves the operation count twice
About 8× fewer — full joint costs ~4.2M pairwise ops; divided temporal + spatial costs ~540K
About 100× fewer — video models are completely intractable with full joint attention at any resolution
questions.explanation: Full joint: (T·H·W)² = (8·256)² ≈ 4.2M. Divided: H·W·T² + T·(H·W)² = 256·64 + 8·65536 ≈ 540K. Ratio ≈ 7.75×. For production video (T=150, H·W=2700), the doc shows the gap reaches multiple orders of magnitude. This cost difference is why TimeSformer and every 2026 video DiT — Sora, Wan, HunyuanVideo — use divided or window attention rather than full joint attention.

questions.question: DreamerV3 operates entirely in a learned latent space during policy rollouts rather than in pixel space. What is the primary benefit for RL?
options: Pixel-space models cannot learn reward signals

Latent-space rollouts are orders of magnitude cheaper, enabling thousands of imagined policy steps without rendering frames — this is what makes DreamerV3 sample-efficient across 150+ tasks without domain-specific tuning
Latent models always outperform pixel models on video quality metrics like FVD
It avoids the need for an explicit action-conditioning interface
questions.explanation: In DreamerV3 the encoder compresses observations to compact latent states, and the world model transitions (state, action) → next state in that latent space. The agent rolls out thousands of imagined trajectories without decoding pixels. Decoding only happens for visualisation. This compressed simulation is what achieves sample efficiency across 150+ tasks from Atari to proprioceptive control with a single set of hyperparameters.

questions.question: In `VideoPatch3D.forward`, a `nn.Conv3d` with kernel and stride equal to `(patch_t, patch_h, patch_w)` is applied to input `(N, C, T, H, W)`. After reshaping and transposing, what shape is the returned token sequence?
options: (N, T·H·W, C) — raw frame pixels rearranged into a flat sequence
(N, T/patch_t, H/patch_h · W/patch_w, dim) — temporal and spatial kept in separate axes
(N, dim, T/patch_t, H/patch_h, W/patch_w) — still a 5D feature map
(N, (T/patch_t)·(H/patch_h)·(W/patch_w), dim) — all spatio-temporal patches flattened into one token sequence
questions.explanation: `VideoPatch3D` uses a stride-equal-to-kernel 3D conv to produce a `(N, dim, t, h, w)` feature map, then reshapes and transposes to `(N, t·h·w, dim)`. The method returns `(tokens, (t, h, w))` so that `DividedAttentionBlock.forward` knows the grid shape when separating temporal and spatial attention passes. This is the 3D generalisation of ViT's 2D patch embedding.

questions.question: Sora 2's 2026 release advertised better physical plausibility. Which specific failure modes did this target?
options: Colour balance and contrast
Text rendering inside images
Weight, balance, object permanence, cause-and-effect — the model now handles dropped objects, characters colliding, and 'failures on purpose' (a missed jump) more believably than Sora 1
Video length
questions.explanation: Prior-generation video models famously failed on spaghetti-eating, drinking from glasses, and persistent-object scenes — hands would pass through objects, items would disappear mid-action. Sora 2 explicitly advertises improvements on weight, balance, object permanence, and cause-and-effect, measured against internal and public plausibility benchmarks. These are the dominant quality failures the field is still working on.

questions.question: In the emerging robotics stack (VLM + video generation + inverse dynamics), what does the inverse dynamics model do?
options: It generates the next frame
It labels training data
It trains the VLM

It takes a pair (current observation, desired next observation from the video model) and outputs the low-level motor action that would connect them; this closes the loop between imagined rollouts and actual actuation
questions.explanation: The VLM plans, the video model imagines, and the inverse dynamics model turns imagination into motor commands. Given two consecutive observations, inverse dynamics asks: what action produced this transition? This three-component stack lets a robot train largely in a learned simulator, using the video model to generate data and the inverse dynamics model to execute.
